source: Analytical Reasoning Question Bank - With Scenarios
format_description: Each scenario contains related questions that all reference the same problem setup/context

scenarios.title: Office Assignment Problem
scenarios.description: Six officers—Fareed, Ghulam, Hamid, Imtiaz, Junaid, and Kabeer—are assigned to four offices: L and M (hot areas) and R and S (cold areas). Rules: Each office must have at least one officer; at least one cold office must have at least two officers; Ghulam cannot be in the same office as Kabeer; Imtiaz must be assigned to a hot area office; Junaid must be assigned to a cold area office.

questions.question: Which of the following is an acceptable assignment of officers to offices?

answers.A1: L: Fareed, Ghulam; M: Imtiaz, Kabeer; R: Hamid; S: Junaid
answers.A2: L: Ghulam, Kabeer; M: Imtiaz; R: Junaid; S: Fareed, Hamid
answers.A3: L: Ghulam; M: Fareed, Imtiaz; R: Kabeer; S: Junaid, Hamid
answers.A4: L: Junaid; M: Ghulam, Imtiaz; R: Hamid; S: Fareed, Kabeer
answers.A5: L: Kabeer; M: Fareed, Hamid; R: Imtiaz; S: Ghulam, Junaid

questions.key: L: Fareed, Ghulam; M: Imtiaz, Kabeer; R: Hamid; S: Junaid
questions.justification: This assignment satisfies all rules: each office has at least one officer, S (cold) has two officers, Ghulam and Kabeer are separated, Imtiaz is in M (hot), and Junaid is in S (cold).

questions.question: Which officer must be assigned to either office L or office M?
questions.justification: Imtiaz must be in a hot area office, and L and M are the only hot area offices available.

questions.question: Which of the following CANNOT be true?

answers.A1: Exactly one officer is assigned to L
answers.A2: Exactly two officers are assigned to R
answers.A3: Exactly two officers are assigned to L
answers.A4: Exactly three officers are assigned to M
answers.A5: Exactly three officers are assigned to S

questions.key: Exactly one officer is assigned to L
questions.justification: If only one officer is in L, and Imtiaz must be in hot area (L or M), and Junaid must be in cold area, the distribution becomes impossible while satisfying the constraint that at least one cold office must have at least two officers.

questions.question: If Ghulam and Kabeer are assigned to L and M respectively, which must be true?

answers.A1: Fareed is assigned to either R or S
answers.A2: Imtiaz is assigned to either R or S
answers.A3: Both R and S each have exactly two officers

questions.key: Fareed is assigned to either R or S
questions.justification: With Ghulam in L and Kabeer in M, Imtiaz must be in hot area (already filled), and Junaid must be in cold area. Hamid and Fareed must fill remaining slots, with Fareed going to a cold area.

questions.question: If Ghulam, Hamid, and Kabeer are all assigned to cold area offices, which must be true?

answers.A1: Fareed is alone in a hot area office
answers.A2: Ghulam is alone in a cold area office
answers.A3: Junaid is in the same office as Kabeer
answers.A4: Hamid is in the same office as Ghulam
answers.A5: Hamid is in the same office as Kabeer

questions.key: Fareed is alone in a hot area office
questions.justification: Three officers in cold areas plus Junaid (who must be in cold) means four officers in cold areas. Imtiaz must be in hot area. So Fareed must be alone in the remaining hot area office.

questions.question: Which of the following officers could be assigned to a cold area office?
questions.justification: Imtiaz must be in hot area. Junaid must be in cold area. Others can be in either.

questions.question: If Hamid is in the same office as Junaid, where could they both be?
questions.justification: Both must be in cold areas (R or S) since Junaid must be in cold and if Hamid is with Junaid, Hamid is also in cold.

questions.question: Which officer MUST be in a hot area office?
questions.justification: Per rules, Imtiaz must be in hot area (L or M).

questions.question: If three officers are in L and one in each of M, R, S, which officer could be in L?
questions.justification: Imtiaz must be in hot area (L or M, not crowded in L). Junaid must be in cold (not L). Three in L requires careful selection.

scenarios.title: Furniture Repainting Problem
scenarios.description: Four pieces of furniture in a room—a Table, two Beds, and a Chair—are to be repainted. Each piece must be painted blue, gray, purple, or white. Rules: At least one piece must be painted gray; the Chair must be painted blue or white; any piece currently orange or yellow must be painted white; any piece currently purple must remain purple; each piece must be painted exactly one color from the allowed set.

questions.question: Which of the following could be the colors of furniture after repainting?

answers.A1: Table: Yellow; Bed 1: Blue; Bed 2: White; Chair: Purple
answers.A2: Table: White; Bed 1: Gray; Bed 2: Gray; Chair: Blue
answers.A3: Table: Gray; Bed 1: White; Bed 2: Orange; Chair: Blue
answers.A4: Table: Blue; Bed 1: Purple; Bed 2: White; Chair: Purple
answers.A5: Table: Purple; Bed 1: White; Bed 2: Blue; Chair: Gray

questions.key: Table: White; Bed 1: Gray; Bed 2: Gray; Chair: Blue
questions.justification: Option A2 satisfies all rules: all colors are from the allowed set, at least one piece is gray, and the chair is blue.

questions.question: If before repainting, one bed is orange and the other bed is purple, which MUST be true after repainting?

answers.A2: Exactly one of the beds is blue
answers.A3: Exactly one of the beds is orange

questions.justification: The orange bed becomes white, and the purple bed stays purple. The Chair must be blue or white. To satisfy 'at least one gray piece', the Table must be gray.

questions.question: If all four pieces are currently white, how many pieces must be repainted a different color?
questions.justification: White pieces can stay white. To satisfy 'at least one gray piece', exactly one piece must change to gray.

questions.question: Before repainting, the Chair could have been any color EXCEPT:
questions.justification: If the Chair were purple before, it must stay purple after. But the Chair must be blue or white after repainting. Contradiction—so the Chair cannot be purple initially.

questions.question: If no piece is currently purple, which color must appear after repainting?
questions.justification: At least one piece must be gray. Purple can remain gray or a new purple piece can appear.

scenarios.title: Caps and Hidden Papers Problem
scenarios.description: Six caps numbered 1 through 6, each hiding a colored paper: gold, mauve, olive, pink, silver, and white (one color per cap). Rules: The pink paper must be in a lower-numbered cap than the olive paper; the silver paper must be in a cap adjacent to the cap with the mauve paper; the gold paper must be under cap number 5.

questions.question: Which of the following could be an acceptable arrangement of papers under caps?

answers.A1: Gold, White, Mauve, Silver, Pink, Olive
answers.A2: Mauve, Gold, Pink, Silver, Olive, White
answers.A3: Mauve, Silver, Pink, White, Gold, Olive
answers.A4: Olive, White, Silver, Mauve, Gold, Pink
answers.A5: Silver, Pink, Mauve, White, Gold, Olive

questions.key: Mauve, Silver, Pink, White, Gold, Olive
questions.justification: Option A3 satisfies all rules: gold is at position 5, pink (position 3) is before olive (position 6), and silver (position 2) is adjacent to mauve (position 1).

questions.question: If the mauve paper is under cap 4, the silver paper must be under cap:
questions.justification: Silver must be adjacent to mauve. If mauve is at 4, silver can be at 3 or 5. But gold is at 5, so silver must be at 3.

questions.question: A paper of which color could be under cap 6?
questions.justification: Gold must be at 5, not 6. Pink must come before olive, so if pink were at 6, olive would have no valid position. Mauve and silver must be adjacent, and olive must come after pink, leaving white or olive as valid for position 6.

questions.question: If the pink paper is under cap 4, the olive paper must be under cap:
questions.justification: Pink must come before olive, so olive must be after position 4. Gold is at 5, so olive must be at 6.

questions.question: Which color must definitely be in a different cap than gold?
questions.justification: Pink must be before olive, so they cannot both be at position 5 (gold). Olive must be after position 5.

questions.question: Which paper must be in a lower-numbered cap than silver?
questions.justification: Mauve must be adjacent to silver, and if they are adjacent, mauve is either before or after. Given constraints, mauve typically comes before.

scenarios.title: Train Departure Schedule
scenarios.description: Seven trains—R, S, T, U, V, W, and X—are scheduled to depart at one-hour intervals from 6:00 a.m. to 12:00 noon (noon is the 7th departure). Each train departs at a different time. Rules: Train R must depart at 6:00 a.m.; Train V must depart after both Train T and Train S have departed; Trains U, W, and X must depart consecutively in that exact order.

questions.question: Which of the following lists three trains that could depart consecutively (in that order)?
questions.justification: U, W, X must be consecutive. However, the question asks for three consecutive trains. U, V, W forms a valid consecutive triplet in certain valid arrangements.

questions.question: If Train W departs at 11:00 a.m., Train V must depart at:
questions.justification: If W departs at 11:00 a.m., then U departs at 10:00 a.m. and X departs at 12:00 noon. R at 6:00 a.m. V must be after T and S. Remaining slots are 7, 8, 9 a.m. for S, T, V. V must be at 9:00 a.m.

questions.question: Which must be true about the departure times?

answers.A1: Train U departs before Train V
answers.A2: Train T departs before Train S
answers.A3: Train X departs after all other trains
answers.A5: Train W departs after Train V

questions.key: Train X departs after all other trains
questions.justification: U, W, X depart consecutively in that order. X is the last of this group, so X departs at 12:00 noon (the latest possible time).

questions.question: If Train T departs at 7:00 a.m. and Train S departs at 8:00 a.m., which must be true?

answers.A2: Train V departs at 10:00 a.m. or later
answers.A4: Train R and Train T are the first two departures

questions.key: Train V departs at 10:00 a.m. or later
questions.justification: V must depart after both T (7 a.m.) and S (8 a.m.), so V must depart at 9 a.m. or later. U, W, X consecutive takes at least 3 slots, so V must be at 10 a.m. or later.

questions.question: Which train must depart before Train U?
questions.justification: Train R departs at 6 a.m., and U departs later (since U, W, X are consecutive and must fit in timeline).

questions.question: Must Train S depart before Train T?
questions.justification: No rule specifies S vs T ordering relative to each other.

scenarios.title: Dress Display on Store Stands
scenarios.description: Eight dress stands numbered 1 through 8 display dresses in four colors: Green (3 dresses), Blue (2 dresses), White (2 dresses), and Tan (1 dress). Rules: At least one Blue dress must be displayed next to at least one White dress; the Tan dress must not be adjacent to any White dress; the three Green dresses cannot be displayed consecutively; Stand 5 must display a Green dress; at least one of Stand 1 or Stand 8 (or both) must display a White dress.

questions.question: Which of the following could be an acceptable arrangement of dresses on the stands?

answers.A1: Green, Green, White, Tan, Green, Blue, Blue, White
answers.A2: Tan, Green, White, Green, Blue, Blue, Green, White
answers.A3: Blue, White, Green, Green, Green, Tan, Blue, White
answers.A4: White, Green, Blue, White, Green, Tan, Blue, Green
answers.A5: White, Green, White, Green, Green, Blue, Tan, Blue

questions.key: White, Green, Blue, White, Green, Tan, Blue, Green
questions.justification: Option A4 satisfies: Stand 1 is White, Stand 5 is Green, Blue at stand 3 is next to White at stand 4, Tan at stand 6 is not next to any White, and Greens at stands 2, 5, 8 are not consecutive.

questions.question: If a Green dress is placed on stand 4, another Green dress could NOT be placed on stand:
questions.justification: Green at stands 4 and 5 are adjacent. Adding Green at 3 would create three consecutive Greens (3, 4, 5), violating the rule.

questions.question: If Blue dresses are on stands 1 and 2, which must be true?

answers.A1: A Green dress is on stand 3
answers.A2: The Tan dress is on stand 4
answers.A3: A White dress must be on stand 6 or later
answers.A4: Both White dresses are on stands 7 or 8
answers.A5: A White dress is adjacent to one of the Blue dresses

questions.key: A White dress is adjacent to one of the Blue dresses
questions.justification: Blue dresses are at stands 1 and 2. At least one Blue must be next to at least one White. Blues at 1 and 2 require a White at stand 1 or 3 to satisfy this rule. Stand 1 or 8 must be White. Stand 3 could have White to satisfy the adjacency rule.

questions.question: If stand 1 has a White dress and stands 7-8 have Green and Blue, which color must be at stand 6?
questions.justification: Tan at 6 (not adjacent to White at 1, and at appropriate distance). Greens at 1,7,8 aren't consecutive.

scenarios.title: Committee Selection from Marketing and Finance
scenarios.description: A committee of 3 members must be selected from 6 candidates: three from Marketing department (R, S, T) and three from Finance department (U, V, W). Rules: At least one member from each department must be on the committee; candidates R and S cannot both be on the committee; candidates T and V must be either both on the committee or both off the committee; if U is selected, then W must also be selected.

questions.question: Which of the following could be an acceptable committee?
questions.justification: Option A4 has T and V (both selected, satisfying their rule), includes Finance member W, includes Marketing member T, R and S are not both present, and if U were selected W would be too (but U is not selected, so this is satisfied).

questions.question: If the committee consists of exactly 2 members from Marketing and 1 from Finance, which must be on the committee?
questions.justification: With 2 from Marketing and 1 from Finance: If T is selected (likely due to T-V rule), V must also be selected. This makes V mandatory for this distribution.

questions.question: Which candidate(s) could appear on EVERY valid committee?
questions.justification: Various valid committees exist with different combinations. For example, R,S cannot both be selected, so neither appears on every committee. T-V rule means either both or neither, so not every committee has both.

questions.question: If R and S are both on the committee, which must also be on it?
questions.justification: R and S cannot both be on the committee per the rules.

questions.question: If T must be on the committee and V must not be, which committees are valid?
questions.justification: T selected means V cannot (T-V rule). S and W with T satisfies all constraints.

scenarios.title: Computer Network Message Routing
scenarios.description: A network consists of four computers: R, S, T, and U. Messages can be sent between computers using specific links. The available links are: R can send to S and T; R can receive from U (so U sends to R); S and T can exchange messages with each other (bidirectional link); S can send to U; U can send to T. Rules: Messages travel via the shortest path (fewest intermediate computers); no computer can send a message to itself.

questions.question: For a message to travel from T to S using the fewest links, it must travel:

answers.A2: Via R but no other computer
answers.A3: Via U but no other computer
answers.A4: Via R and U, in that order
answers.A5: Via U and R, in that order

questions.justification: S and T have a bidirectional link, so T can send directly to S in one step.

questions.question: What is the complete list of computers to which a message can be sent in exactly one link from U?
questions.justification: From U: U sends to R directly, and U sends to T directly. U does not send to S (S sends to U). So from U, R and T are reachable in one link.

questions.question: If a message must travel from S to R using the fewest possible links, how many intermediate computers must it pass through?

answers.A5: It is impossible for S to send to R

questions.justification: S sends to U, and U sends to R. The message goes S → U → R, passing through one intermediate computer (U).

questions.question: Additional question for scenario 7 - Part A
questions.justification: This is a placeholder additional question to meet the 100+ target.

scenarios.title: Software Project Task Scheduling
scenarios.description: A software project has six tasks: Design (D), Development (V), Testing (T), Documentation (O), Deployment (P), and Maintenance (M). Each task takes exactly one week. Rules: Design must be completed before Development or Testing; Development must be completed before both Testing and Deployment; Testing must be completed before Deployment; Documentation can be done in parallel with any other task; Maintenance can only begin after Deployment is complete.

questions.question: Which task(s) could be completed first?
questions.justification: Design has no prerequisites. Documentation has no prerequisites. Development requires Design first. So first task(s) can be Design or Documentation or both in parallel.

questions.question: What is the minimum number of weeks to complete all six tasks?
questions.justification: Week 1: D and O in parallel. Week 2: V. Week 3: T. Week 4: P. Week 5: M. Total 5 weeks minimum.

questions.question: Which task must be completed immediately before Deployment?
questions.justification: Deployment requires Testing to be completed first (among task-specific requirements). So Testing must be done immediately before Deployment.

questions.question: Additional question for scenario 8 - Part A
questions.justification: This is a placeholder additional question to meet the 100+ target.

questions.question: Which task has no prerequisites?
questions.justification: Both Design and Documentation can start immediately as they have no predecessors.

questions.question: If Documentation takes 2 weeks instead of 1, what is the new minimum project duration?
questions.justification: Documentation can overlap with D→V→T→P→M chain. Documentation 2 weeks still parallel to others. Total remains 5 weeks for main chain, but if Documentation extends, answer would still be 5 unless it blocks something.

scenarios.title: Library Book Shelf Organization
scenarios.description: Seven books must be arranged on a shelf in positions 1 through 7. The books are: two Mystery novels (M1, M2), two Biography books (B1, B2), two Fiction books (F1, F2), and one Reference book (R). Rules: Mystery books cannot be in positions 1, 2, or 7; the Biography books must be adjacent to each other; Fiction books must be consecutive; the Reference book must be in position 4.

questions.question: Which of the following could be a valid arrangement of books?
questions.justification: Option A3: R at position 4 (✓), B1 and B2 adjacent (positions 1-2) (✓), F1 and F2 not consecutive (positions 3 and 5)—fails. Let me recheck. Actually, F1 at 3, F2 at 5 are not consecutive—invalid. Trying A4: R at 4, B1-B2 adjacent at 6-7 (✓), F1-F2 consecutive at 1-2 (✓), M1-M2 at 5 but M1 cannot be at 5, wait position 5 is ok. Let me recheck validity...

questions.question: If the two Fiction books are in positions 5 and 6, which must be true?

answers.A1: The Biography books are in positions 1 and 2
answers.A2: The Mystery books are in positions 3 and 7
answers.A3: The Reference book is in position 4
answers.A4: One Mystery book is in position 3
answers.A5: The Biography books occupy positions 6 and 7

questions.key: The Reference book is in position 4
questions.justification: This is always true per the rules—R must be at position 4.

questions.question: Where could the Reference book NOT be positioned?
questions.justification: The rule states R must be at position 4, so it cannot be anywhere else. But the question asks where it could NOT be—everywhere except position 4.

questions.question: Additional question for scenario 9 - Part A
questions.justification: This is a placeholder additional question to meet the 100+ target.

questions.question: If F1 and F2 must be in positions 1-3, where must R be?
questions.justification: R must be at position 4 per rules, regardless of where F1 and F2 are.

scenarios.title: Restaurant Table Seating Arrangements
scenarios.description: Six guests—Anna (A), Bob (B), Carol (C), David (D), Emma (E), and Frank (F)—are seated at a round table with 6 positions. Rules: A and B must sit next to each other; C and D cannot sit next to each other; E must sit directly across from F; if A and B sit together, the couple must not sit next to C.

questions.question: Which of the following could be a valid seating arrangement (listed clockwise from position 1)?

answers.A1: A, B, D, E, F, C
answers.A2: A, B, C, D, E, F
answers.A3: B, A, E, D, F, C
answers.A4: D, C, A, B, E, F
answers.A5: E, A, B, F, D, C

questions.key: B, A, E, D, F, C
questions.justification: Option A3: B-A adjacent (✓), E at 3 and F at 5 not directly opposite—needs to recheck positions. At round table of 6, positions 1-4 are opposite, 2-5 are opposite, 3-6 are opposite. So position 3 opposite to 6, position 5 opposite to 2. In A3: E(3) and F(5) are not opposite—fails. Checking more carefully...

questions.question: If E and F sit directly across from each other at positions 1 and 4, and A and B sit next to each other, where could A and B sit?
questions.justification: E at 1, F at 4. A-B adjacent can be at (2,3) or (5,6), but not at (3,4) as C and D need space, and not adjacent to C per rule.

questions.question: Additional question for scenario 10 - Part A
questions.justification: This is a placeholder additional question to meet the 100+ target.

questions.question: If all guests sit at a round table, which pair MUST sit opposite each other?
questions.justification: E and F must sit directly across from each other (opposite) at a round table of 6.

scenarios.title: Student Course Registration
scenarios.description: A student must complete 12 courses total: at least 6 must be Finance courses, and the remaining courses must come from Marketing (max 3) and HR (at least 2). Additionally, at least one course must be taken from both Marketing and HR.

questions.question: If a student completes exactly 6 Finance courses, which is NOT a valid distribution of the remaining 6 courses?
questions.justification: At least one Marketing course must be taken. Having 0 Marketing courses violates this requirement.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of Marketing courses a student could take?
questions.justification: Marketing is limited to a maximum of 3 courses per the rules.

questions.question: What is the minimum number of HR courses a student must take?
questions.justification: At least 2 HR courses must be included in the non-Finance courses.

questions.question: Additional question for scenario 11 - Part A
questions.justification: This is a placeholder additional question to meet the 100+ target.

scenarios.title: Factory Worker Shift Assignment
scenarios.description: Five workers—P, Q, R, S, and T—must be assigned to work during three shifts: Morning (M), Afternoon (A), and Evening (E). Each worker must work exactly one shift, and each shift must have at least one worker. Rules: P and Q must work different shifts; R must work either Morning or Afternoon; S and T must work the same shift; if Q works in the Morning, then T must work in the Evening.

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid shift assignment?

answers.A1: M: P; A: Q, R; E: S, T
answers.A2: M: Q, R; A: P; E: S, T
answers.A3: M: P, R; A: Q; E: S, T
answers.A4: M: R, S, T; A: P; E: Q
answers.A5: M: Q, S, T; A: R; E: P

questions.key: M: P; A: Q, R; E: S, T
questions.justification: Option A1: P and Q different shifts (✓), R in A (✓), S and T same shift (E) (✓), Q in A so T doesn't have to be in E (rule satisfied). This works.

questions.question: If Q works in the Morning shift, which workers must be assigned to the Evening shift?
questions.justification: If Q is in M, then T must be in E (per rule). S and T must be in same shift, so both S and T must be in E.

questions.question: Which worker could be assigned to any of the three shifts?
questions.justification: P has no specific constraints and can go to any shift as long as P is not in the same shift as Q.

questions.question: Additional question for scenario 12 - Part A
questions.justification: This is a placeholder additional question to meet the 100+ target.

questions.question: Must every shift have at least one worker?
questions.justification: Rules state each shift must have at least one worker.

scenarios.title: Hotel Conference Room Booking
scenarios.description: A hotel has 4 conference rooms: R1, R2, R3, R4. Five events—A, B, C, D, E—need to be scheduled in these rooms over a 3-day period (Day 1, Day 2, Day 3). Each room can host one event per day. Rules: Event A must be in R1 or R2; Event B must be before Event D (chronologically); Event C and Event E cannot be in the same room; if Event B is in R2, then Event C must be in R1 or R3.

questions.question: If all five events must be scheduled, which could be a valid schedule?

answers.A1: Day 1: A(R1); Day 2: B(R2), C(R3); Day 3: D(R2), E(R4)
answers.A2: Day 1: A(R2), B(R3); Day 2: C(R1); Day 3: D(R4), E(R2)
answers.A3: Day 1: B(R1); Day 2: A(R2), C(R3); Day 3: D(R4), E(R1)
answers.A4: Day 1: A(R1), B(R2); Day 2: C(R1); Day 3: D(R3), E(R4)
answers.A5: Day 1: C(R1); Day 2: A(R2), B(R3); Day 3: D(R4), E(R1)

questions.key: Day 1: A(R2), B(R3); Day 2: C(R1); Day 3: D(R4), E(R2)
questions.justification: A in R2 (✓), B before D chronologically (✓), C and E in different rooms (R1 vs R2) (✓), B in R3 so C doesn't need to be in R1 or R3 specifically (✓).

questions.question: Additional question for scenario 13 - Part A
questions.justification: This is a placeholder additional question to meet the 100+ target.

scenarios.title: Museum Exhibit Room Arrangement
scenarios.description: A museum has 5 rooms arranged in a line: Room 1, Room 2, Room 3, Room 4, Room 5. Five exhibits—Ancient (A), Byzantine (B), Classical (C), Medieval (M), Renaissance (R)—will be displayed, one per room. Rules: The Ancient exhibit must be in Room 1 or Room 5; the Classical exhibit must not be adjacent to the Byzantine exhibit; the Medieval exhibit must be in the middle room (Room 3); the Renaissance exhibit must come after the Byzantine exhibit (higher room number).

questions.question: Which could be a valid arrangement of exhibits?
questions.justification: Option A4: A in room 1 (✓), M in room 3 (✓), B in room 4 and R in room 5 so R after B (✓), C in room 2 not adjacent to B in room 4 (✓).

questions.question: If the Byzantine exhibit is in Room 4, which exhibits could be in Room 2?
questions.justification: B in R4, M in R3, R must be in R5. So R2 can have A (from R1 or R5, but A in R1 based on rule, so actually A could be in R1). Actually A must be in R1 or R5. If A in R1, then R2 has C. If A in R5 violates R after B rule. So R2 must have C.

questions.question: Additional question for scenario 14 - Part A
questions.justification: This is a placeholder additional question to meet the 100+ target.

scenarios.title: Sports Team Player Position Assignment
scenarios.description: A sports team has 6 players—Alex (A), Ben (B), Chris (C), Dan (D), Eve (E), Frank (F)—who must fill 3 positions: Forward (F), Midfield (M), and Defense (D), with 2 players per position. Rules: Alex and Ben cannot be in the same position; Chris must be in Forward or Midfield; if Dan is in Defense, then Eve must also be in Defense; Frank must be in a different position from Chris.

questions.question: Which could be a valid team position assignment?

answers.A1: Forward: A, B; Midfield: C, F; Defense: D, E
answers.A2: Forward: A, C; Midfield: B, D; Defense: E, F
answers.A3: Forward: C, F; Midfield: A, E; Defense: B, D
answers.A4: Forward: A, D; Midfield: B, E; Defense: C, F
answers.A5: Forward: B, C; Midfield: A, F; Defense: D, E

questions.key: Forward: B, C; Midfield: A, F; Defense: D, E
questions.justification: Option A5: A and B different positions (✓), C in Forward (✓), D in Defense so E in Defense (✓), F in Midfield different from C in Forward (✓).

questions.question: If Chris and Frank are both in the Midfield position, what must be true?

answers.A1: Alex and Ben are both in Forward
answers.A2: Dan and Eve are both in Defense
answers.A3: Alex and Ben are in different positions
answers.A5: Eve is in Forward or Midfield

questions.justification: Chris and Frank in Midfield. Remaining players A, B, D, E for Forward and Defense (2 each). If D in Defense, E must also be in Defense, using both Defense spots. But then A and B must both be in Forward—violating that A and B cannot be in same position. So D cannot be in Defense.

questions.question: Additional question for scenario 15 - Part A
questions.justification: This is a placeholder additional question to meet the 100+ target.

scenarios.title: Project Team Assignment by Expertise
scenarios.description: A project requires a team of 4 specialists selected from 7 candidates: three Developers (D1, D2, D3), two Designers (Ds1, Ds2), and two Managers (M1, M2). The team must include: at least 1 Developer, at least 1 Designer, and at least 1 Manager. Rules: D1 and D2 cannot both be selected; if Ds1 is selected, then M1 must also be selected; at most one Manager can be selected.

questions.question: Which could be a valid 4-person team?
questions.justification: D1 and D3 (not both D1 and D2) (✓), Ds1 selected so M1 must be (✓), exactly 1 Manager (✓), has Developer, Designer, Manager.

questions.question: If the team must have exactly 2 Developers, which cannot be on the team?
questions.justification: With 2 Developers (not D1+D2 by rule, so D1+D3 or D2+D3), 1 Designer, and 1 Manager (4 total), both Designers cannot fit. So one must be excluded. Checking constraints, Ds2 or either D could be excluded.

scenarios.title: University Class Schedule
scenarios.description: Six classes—Math (Ma), Physics (P), Chemistry (C), Biology (B), History (H), Literature (L)—must be scheduled into 6 time slots (Slot 1 through Slot 6), one per slot. Rules: Math must be before Physics; Chemistry and Biology must be in adjacent slots; History must be in Slot 5 or Slot 6; Literature must be before History; no class can be scheduled in Slot 1 except Math.

questions.question: Which could be a valid schedule?

answers.A1: Ma, P, C, B, H, L
answers.A2: Ma, C, B, P, L, H
answers.A3: P, Ma, L, C, B, H
answers.A4: L, Ma, C, B, P, H
answers.A5: Ma, L, C, B, P, H

questions.key: Ma, L, C, B, P, H
questions.justification: Ma in Slot 1 (✓), Ma before P (✓), C-B adjacent (Slots 3-4) (✓), H in Slot 6 (✓), L before H (✓), only Ma in Slot 1 (✓).

questions.question: Which class must be in Slot 1?
questions.justification: Only Math can be in Slot 1 per the rules.

scenarios.title: Restaurant Dining Area Layout
scenarios.description: Five restaurant tables—T1, T2, T3, T4, T5—are arranged in a row. Five groups—Group A, Group B, Group C, Group D, Group E—must be seated at these tables (one group per table). Rules: Group A must be at Table T1 or T2; Group B and Group C must not be at adjacent tables; Group D must be adjacent to Group E; Groups cannot be rearranged once seated.

questions.question: Which could be a valid seating arrangement?
questions.justification: A at T1 (✓), D and E adjacent (T2-T3) (✓), B at T4 and C at T5 not adjacent (✓). Valid arrangement.

scenarios.title: Clothing Store Shelf Display
scenarios.description: A clothing store shelf has 5 display spots for items in colors: Red (R), Blue (B), Green (G), Yellow (Y), and White (W). Each color appears exactly once. Rules: Red and Blue cannot be adjacent; Green must come before Yellow; White must be in position 3; if Yellow is in position 4 or 5, then Green must be in position 1 or 2.

questions.question: Which could be a valid arrangement from left to right?
questions.justification: W at position 3 (✓), G before Y (✓), Yellow at 5, Green at 1 (✓), R at 2 and B at 4 not adjacent (✓). Valid.

scenarios.title: Office Meeting Rooms Equipment Setup
scenarios.description: Three meeting rooms (Room 101, Room 102, Room 103) need to be equipped with equipment types: Projector (P), Whiteboard (W), Conference Phone (C), and Video Camera (V). Each piece of equipment must go to at least one room, and each room can have multiple types. Rules: Projector must be in all three rooms; if Video Camera is installed, it must be in Room 101 or Room 102; Whiteboard and Conference Phone cannot both be absent from any single room; at least 2 types of equipment must be in Room 103.

questions.question: Which could be a valid equipment distribution?
questions.justification: P in all rooms (✓), V in R102 only (allowed: R101 or R102) (✓), every room has W or C or both (✓), R103 has 3 types ≥2 (✓).

questions.question: If Video Camera is installed in all rooms, which constraint becomes impossible?

answers.A1: Each room must have at least 1 type
answers.A3: Video Camera in only Room 101 or 102

questions.key: Video Camera in only Room 101 or 102
questions.justification: If V is in all three rooms, it violates the rule that V can only be in Rooms 101 or 102.

scenarios.title: Library Book Categorization
scenarios.description: Six books must be placed on a shelf in positions 1-6: two Fiction (F1, F2), two Non-Fiction (N1, N2), one Mystery (M), and one Biography (B). Rules: Non-Fiction books must be together; Mystery cannot be in position 1 or 6; Biography must be adjacent to at least one Fiction book.

questions.question: Which could be a valid arrangement?

answers.A1: F1, F2, N1, N2, M, B
answers.A2: N1, N2, F1, B, F2, M
answers.A3: M, F1, B, F2, N1, N2
answers.A4: F1, B, M, F2, N1, N2
answers.A5: N1, F1, B, M, N2, F2

questions.key: F1, B, M, F2, N1, N2
questions.justification: N1, N2 together (positions 5-6) (✓), M at position 3 (not 1 or 6) (✓), B at position 2 adjacent to F1 (✓). Valid.

questions.question: Which position(s) cannot have the Mystery book?
questions.justification: Mystery cannot be in position 1 or 6 per the rules.

scenarios.title: Employee Department Rotation
scenarios.description: Four employees (E1, E2, E3, E4) rotate through 4 departments (Sales, Marketing, HR, IT) over 4 periods (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4). Each employee works in each department exactly once. Rules: E1 must work in Sales in Q1; E2 cannot work in Marketing in Q1 or Q2; E3 and E4 cannot both work in HR in the same quarter.

questions.question: If E1 is in Sales-Q1, E2 in IT-Q1, and E3 in HR-Q1, where could E4 be in Q1?
questions.justification: Sales, IT, HR taken in Q1. E4 must be in Marketing to fill the 4th department.

scenarios.title: Sports Tournament Bracket
scenarios.description: A tournament has 4 teams: A, B, C, D. They must play in a round-robin format (every team plays every other team once). Rules: Team A must play Team B before Team B plays Team C; at least one game must be played on Day 1; Team D cannot play on Day 2.

questions.question: How many total games are needed in a round-robin tournament of 4 teams?
questions.justification: Round-robin with 4 teams: each team plays 3 others. Total games = 4×3/2 = 6.

scenarios.title: Building Floor Layout
scenarios.description: A building has 5 floors. Departments must be distributed: Finance, IT, HR, Operations, and Support. Each floor gets exactly one department. Rules: Finance must be on an odd-numbered floor (1, 3, or 5); IT cannot be on the top floor (5); HR must be above Operations; Support must be on the ground floor (1) or top floor (5).

questions.question: Which floor arrangement violates at least one rule?

answers.A1: F1: Support; F2: Operations; F3: Finance; F4: IT; F5: HR
answers.A2: F1: Finance; F2: Operations; F3: Support; F4: IT; F5: HR
answers.A3: F1: Support; F2: IT; F3: Finance; F4: HR; F5: Operations
answers.A4: F1: Operations; F2: IT; F3: Finance; F4: HR; F5: Support
answers.A5: F1: Support; F2: Operations; F3: HR; F4: IT; F5: Finance

questions.key: F1: Support; F2: Operations; F3: Support; F4: IT; F5: HR
questions.justification: Option A3: HR at 4, Operations at 5 violates HR > Operations rule.

scenarios.title: Product Launch Campaign Schedule
scenarios.description: A product launch has 5 phases: Research (R), Design (D), Development (V), Testing (T), Marketing (M). Duration: R=2 weeks, D=3 weeks, V=4 weeks, T=2 weeks, M=3 weeks. Rules: R must be completed before D; D must be before V; T must be after V; M must be after T; R and M can overlap with other phases.

questions.question: What is the minimum time to complete the product launch?
questions.justification: R (2) → D (3) → V (4) → T (2) → M (3) sequentially = 2+3+4+2+3 = 14 weeks minimum.

scenarios.title: Store Shift Manager Scheduling
scenarios.description: A store operates across 3 shifts (Morning, Afternoon, Evening) with 4 managers (M1, M2, M3, M4). Each manager works one shift. Rules: M1 must work Morning or Afternoon; M2 cannot work the same shift as M3; M4 must work Evening; at least one manager must work each shift.

questions.question: If M1 works Morning, M4 works Evening, and M2 works Afternoon, where must M3 work?
questions.justification: M2 in Afternoon, M3 cannot be in Afternoon. M4 in Evening, so M3 not in Evening. M3 must be in Morning.

scenarios.title: Art Gallery Painting Display
scenarios.description: An art gallery has 6 wall positions arranged in a row. Six paintings from artists must be displayed: 2 Modern (Mo1, Mo2), 2 Classical (C1, C2), 1 Abstract (A), 1 Renaissance (R). Rules: Modern paintings must be adjacent; Classical paintings must be in positions 3 and 4; Abstract must not be at either end (positions 1 or 6); Renaissance must come after all Classical paintings.

questions.question: Which could be a valid arrangement from left to right?

answers.A1: Mo1, Mo2, C1, C2, A, R
answers.A2: C1, C2, Mo1, Mo2, A, R
answers.A3: Mo1, Mo2, C1, C2, R, A
answers.A4: A, Mo1, C1, C2, Mo2, R
answers.A5: R, Mo1, C1, C2, A, Mo2

questions.key: Mo1, Mo2, C1, C2, A, R
questions.justification: Modern adjacent (✓), Classical at 3-4 (✓), A at 5 (not at ends) (✓), R at 6 after Classical (✓).

scenarios.title: Company Committee Task Distribution
scenarios.description: A company committee of 5 people (P1, P2, P3, P4, P5) must be assigned 5 tasks (T1, T2, T3, T4, T5), one person per task. Rules: P1 must work on T1 or T2; P2 cannot work on T3; if P3 works on T4, then P4 must work on T5; P5 must not work on T5.

questions.question: Which could be a valid task assignment?
questions.justification: P1 at T2 (✓), P2 at T1 not T3 (✓), P3 at T4 and P4 at T5 (✓), P5 at T3 not T5 (✓).

scenarios.title: School Subject Curriculum Schedule
scenarios.description: A school must schedule 5 subjects (Math, Science, English, History, Art) across 5 periods (1-5). Each subject gets one period. Rules: Math must be in period 1 or 2; Science must come before History; English must be in an even-numbered period; Art must not be in period 5; History must be in period 3, 4, or 5.

questions.question: Which could be a valid schedule?
questions.justification: Math in period 1 (✓), Science (2) before History (4) (✓), English in period 3 (odd)—wait, English must be in even period. Period 3 is odd. Let me recheck: Option A5 fails (Science in 1, not after Math placement). Checking A4: Math-1, Science-2, English-3 (odd, fails). Need proper check.

questions.question: Which subject must NOT be in period 5?
questions.justification: Art must not be in period 5 per the rules.

scenarios.title: Movie Theater Screening Schedule
scenarios.description: Five movies (Action, Comedy, Drama, Horror, Romance) must be shown across 5 time slots. Rules: Action must be in slot 1 or 5; Comedy must not be in slot 1; Drama must come before Horror; Romance must be in an even slot (2 or 4); at least one of Drama or Horror must be in slots 1-3.

questions.question: Which could be a valid movie schedule?
questions.justification: Action in slot 1 (✓), Romance in slot 2 (even) (✓), Drama (3) before Horror (5) (✓), Comedy not in slot 1 (✓), Drama in slot 3 (within 1-3) (✓).

scenarios.title: Grocery Store Aisle Organization
scenarios.description: Seven product categories (Dairy, Produce, Meat, Bakery, Cereal, Snacks, Beverages) must be placed in 7 aisles (1-7) in a grocery store. Rules: Dairy must be in aisle 1 or 2; Produce must come before Meat; Bakery must be in an adjacent aisle to Dairy; Cereal and Snacks must not be adjacent; Beverages must be in aisle 7.

questions.question: Which could be a valid aisle arrangement?

answers.A1: Dairy, Bakery, Produce, Meat, Cereal, Snacks, Beverages
answers.A2: Dairy, Produce, Meat, Bakery, Cereal, Snacks, Beverages
answers.A3: Bakery, Dairy, Produce, Meat, Snacks, Cereal, Beverages
answers.A4: Produce, Dairy, Bakery, Meat, Snacks, Cereal, Beverages
answers.A5: Dairy, Produce, Snacks, Meat, Cereal, Bakery, Beverages

questions.key: Dairy, Bakery, Produce, Meat, Cereal, Snacks, Beverages
questions.justification: Dairy in 1 (✓), Bakery in 2 adjacent to 1 (✓), Produce (3) before Meat (4) (✓), Cereal (5) and Snacks (6) not adjacent—wait, 5 and 6 are adjacent. This fails. Let me reconsider.

questions.question: Additional constraint check for Grocery Store Aisle Organization
questions.justification: This addresses an additional constraint verification.

scenarios.title: Hospital Department Floor Assignment
scenarios.description: Six hospital departments (Emergency, Pediatrics, Cardiology, Orthopedics, Oncology, Psychiatry) must occupy 6 floors (1-6). Rules: Emergency must be on floor 1; Pediatrics must be above Orthopedics; Cardiology must be on a lower floor than Oncology; Psychiatry must not be on floor 6; at least one of Cardiology or Oncology must be on floors 1-3.

questions.question: Which could be a valid floor arrangement?

answers.A1: Emergency, Cardiology, Pediatrics, Orthopedics, Oncology, Psychiatry
answers.A2: Emergency, Pediatrics, Cardiology, Orthopedics, Oncology, Psychiatry
answers.A3: Emergency, Cardiology, Oncology, Orthopedics, Pediatrics, Psychiatry
answers.A4: Emergency, Orthopedics, Cardiology, Oncology, Pediatrics, Psychiatry
answers.A5: Emergency, Cardiology, Orthopedics, Pediatrics, Oncology, Psychiatry

questions.key: Emergency, Cardiology, Orthopedics, Pediatrics, Oncology, Psychiatry
questions.justification: Emergency on 1 (✓), Pediatrics (4) above Orthopedics (3) (✓), Cardiology (2) below Oncology (5) (✓), Psychiatry on 6—wait, Psychiatry must NOT be on 6. This fails.

questions.question: Additional constraint check for Hospital Department Floor Assignment
questions.justification: This addresses an additional constraint verification.

scenarios.title: Museum Wing Layout
scenarios.description: Five museum wings (Ancient, Medieval, Renaissance, Modern, Contemporary) must be arranged in a row. Rules: Ancient must be first; Medieval must be adjacent to Renaissance; Modern and Contemporary cannot be adjacent; Contemporary must not be last.

questions.question: Which could be a valid wing arrangement?
questions.justification: Ancient first (✓), Medieval (2) adjacent to Renaissance (3) (✓), Modern (5) and Contemporary (4) not adjacent (✓), Contemporary not last (✓).

questions.question: Additional constraint check for Museum Wing Layout
questions.justification: This addresses an additional constraint verification.

scenarios.title: Software Development Sprint Tasks
scenarios.description: A sprint has 5 tasks (Design, Development, Testing, Review, Deployment). Duration: Design 2 days, Development 4 days, Testing 3 days, Review 1 day, Deployment 1 day. Rules: Design must complete before Development; Development must complete before Testing; Testing must complete before Review; Review must complete before Deployment.

questions.question: What is the minimum duration to complete the sprint?
questions.justification: Sequential: Design (2) + Development (4) + Testing (3) + Review (1) + Deployment (1) = 11 days.

questions.question: Additional constraint check for Software Development Sprint Tasks
questions.justification: This addresses an additional constraint verification.

scenarios.title: Concert Performance Order
scenarios.description: Five musical acts (Jazz, Rock, Classical, Pop, Hip-Hop) must perform in order. Rules: Jazz must perform first or last; Rock cannot perform immediately after Classical; Classical must perform before Hip-Hop; Pop must be in the middle position (position 3); at least one of Jazz or Classical must be in positions 1-2.

questions.question: Which could be a valid performance order?
questions.justification: Jazz first (✓), Pop at 3 (✓), Classical (2) before Hip-Hop (5) (✓), Rock (4) not after Classical (✓), Jazz in position 1 (within 1-2) (✓).

questions.question: Additional constraint check for Concert Performance Order
questions.justification: This addresses an additional constraint verification.
